---
title: "Audio Capture and Session Lifecycle"
---

## Why AUHAL

`MicrophoneCaptureService` uses the macOS Audio Unit Hardware Abstraction Layer,
not the simpler default-input APIs. AUHAL permits the application to select a
specific input device without changing the user's system-wide default.

The configuration sequence is:

1. find the HAL output component;
2. instantiate an `AudioUnit`;
3. enable input bus 1;
4. disable output bus 0;
5. select the Core Audio device ID;
6. query the device's native stream format;
7. create an `AVAudioConverter`;
8. install an input callback;
9. initialize and start the unit.

## Real-time callback discipline

Core Audio invokes `microphoneInputCallback`. A real-time callback should avoid
blocking, model inference, file I/O, UI work, and unbounded allocation.

The callback:

1. allocates an `AVAudioPCMBuffer` for the delivered frames;
2. calls `AudioUnitRender`;
3. converts to the application's fixed output format;
4. dispatches the resulting `Data` to a serial processing queue;
5. returns to Core Audio.

The fixed model format is:

```text
signed PCM16, little endian, 16,000 samples/s, one channel
```

This is the contract consumed by `ccv_stream_feed_pcm16`.

## Resampling

The input device may operate at 44.1 kHz, 48 kHz, multiple channels, or a
floating-point representation. `AVAudioConverter` handles sample-rate and
format conversion.

For an input buffer with \(N\) frames:

\[
N_{out} \approx \left\lceil
N \frac{16000}{f_{in}}
\right\rceil
\text{margin}
\]

The implementation adds 32 frames of capacity to avoid truncation caused by
converter delay or rounding.

## Capture gate and pause semantics

The microphone hardware remains open while paused so resume is immediate.
However, a `Mutex<Bool>` gate is checked at the first line of the processing
handler. When false, a chunk is neither:

- written to the archive;
- counted in diagnostics;
- sent to Voxtral;
- used for voice activity.

Pausing also requests a model boundary so buffered pre-pause audio cannot be
joined to speech after resume. This is stricter than merely asking the domain
model to ignore captions: it protects the recording and inference input too.

## Starting a session

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant UI
    participant App as ClassroomAppModel
    participant Vox as Voxtral service
    participant Mic as Microphone service
    participant Arc as Archive

    UI->>App: Start Session
    App->>Vox: load model and create stream
    Vox->>Vox: 3.2 s silent pre-roll
    Vox-->>App: connected
    App->>Mic: request permission
    App->>Arc: create session directory and WAV
    App->>Mic: configure and start AUHAL
    App->>App: phase = listening; audio gate = true
```

The microphone opens only after Voxtral has loaded and completed its silent
pre-roll. This ordering fixes the common startup bug in which the first words
are consumed while the decoder constructs its initial audio prompt.

## Voice activity and boundaries

For embedded Voxtral, every chunk is measured:

\[
\text{RMS} =
\sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}x_i^2}
\]

RMS is converted to a visual level over an approximate \([-60,0]\) dB range.
When normalized level exceeds `0.06`, a 900 ms timer is restarted. If no later
active chunk cancels it, the timer flushes Voxtral and finalizes the current
caption.

This is a deliberately simple endpoint detector. It is not a learned VAD.

## Stopping safely

Stop is a multi-stage operation:

1. phase becomes `stopping`;
2. audio gate closes;
3. capture stops and queued audio callbacks drain;
4. Voxtral finishes and returns any final provisional text;
5. pending Gemma corrections are allowed up to 60 seconds;
6. the session transitions to `idle`;
7. the WAV header is rewritten with the final byte count;
8. transcripts and metadata are atomically written.

`stopping` accepts one final caption because `vox_stream_finish` can expose text
that was not available before the last padding.

## WAV construction

The archive initially writes a 44-byte WAV header with a zero data length, then
appends PCM bytes. At finalization it seeks to byte zero and replaces the header.

Important RIFF fields are:

| Field | Value |
| --- | --- |
| format | PCM (`1`) |
| channels | `1` |
| sample rate | `16000` |
| bits/sample | `16` |
| byte rate | `16000 * 1 * 2 = 32000` |
| block align | `2` |
| data size | final PCM byte count |

Classic RIFF stores sizes in 32 bits, so the service rejects audio above the
4 GiB format limit rather than writing a corrupt file.
